source.title: Goals and habits in the brain

confidence_rationale: Neuron review by two of the field's leading figures, surveying four generations of empirical work on goal-directed vs habit dichotomy. Argues that the dichotomy is better understood as a SPECTRUM with intermediate levels, and that the strict two-system framing of Daw 2005 has been complicated by subsequent evidence (e.g., model-based and model-free systems share circuitry, Pavlovian-to-instrumental transfer reveals additional levels, the dichotomy may be parametric rather than discrete). Confidence 0.82: high source quality, definitive review of the field's state, directly informs whether REE should commit to two-system or richer architecture.
failure_signatures: Dolan & Dayan argue the dichotomy is more like a spectrum or a continuum than two discrete systems. An REE architectural commitment to 'two and only two systems' (habit cache + goal-directed planner) would over-simplify. The paper licenses a richer architecture but does not specify exactly how many levels or what the intermediate substrates are.

The 'fifth generation' work the paper points to (model-based / model-free interactions, Pavlovian-to-instrumental transfer, mixed strategies) is recent enough that the architectural picture is still evolving. Any REE substrate decision based on Dolan-Dayan's reading should remain provisional pending further empirical resolution.

The paper does not address hierarchical / option-level action representation directly. The decomposition Dolan & Dayan discuss is goal-directed vs habit, not goal-directed vs option-level vs habit -- these are orthogonal dimensions that REE's architectural mapping has to resolve.

mapping.source_claim_statement: An enduring and richly elaborated dichotomy in cognitive neuroscience is that of reflective versus reflexive decision making. Other literatures refer to the two ends of what is likely to be a SPECTRUM with terms such as goal-directed versus habitual, model-based versus model-free, or prospective versus retrospective. Four generations of experimental work have refined the picture; new paradigms in the fifth generation are probing and challenging the very heart of the distinction, suggesting the systems share circuitry and interact rather than competing as cleanly separated subsystems.
mapping.ree_translation: For SD-004 (action-object space) and ARC-021 (three BG-like loops): the canonical biological reading has shifted from 'two discrete systems' (Daw 2005) to 'spectrum with shared circuitry and rich interactions' (Dolan & Dayan 2013). For REE this licenses NOT building a habit-cache and a goal-directed planner as fully separate substrates. Instead, the substrate decision should respect that the systems share circuitry (the same BG, the same PFC, the same HPC -- with different read-write patterns). REE's ARC-021 three-loop framework is consistent with the spectrum reading: three loops sharing input (from cortex) and output (to thalamus), differing in their internal learning channels and timescales. A habit substrate, if added, should integrate WITH the existing planner machinery -- e.g., as a cached lookup that the proposer queries before falling through to CEM rollout, rather than as a parallel system that competes with the proposer.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The 'spectrum' framing is harder to operationalise architecturally than the 'two systems' framing. REE has to commit to specific substrates with specific operators; the spectrum reading licenses richness but does not specify exactly which intermediate substrates to build. A V3 implementation pass would likely simplify to the two-system framing as a starting point and refine toward the spectrum framing as evidence demands.
mapping.source_context: Neuron review article surveying 30+ years of work on goal-directed vs habit dichotomy. Covers behavioural devaluation paradigms, computational model-based / model-free formalism, neural lesion / fMRI evidence, and the most recent work showing shared circuitry and rich interactions between the systems.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high (Neuron, two leading authorities, definitive review). Mapping fidelity moderate-to-high: the spectrum framing is more architecturally honest than the two-system framing but harder to translate directly into REE substrate decisions. Transfer risk modest because the framework is computational/theoretical and translates directly to REE's architectural scaffolding -- the main uncertainty is how to operationalise 'spectrum' rather than whether the framework applies.
